danger_signals.visual_form: Sprite-based predators (lions, bears, snakes, scorpions) in platformer environments
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental hazards that must be avoided or neutralized; represent natural threats and divine obstacles

danger_signals.visual_form: Human enemy sprites (Pharaoh's soldiers in Baby Moses; guards in David and Goliath segments)
danger_signals.meaning: Political/authoritarian threat; represent persecution and human opposition to biblical protagonists

danger_signals.visual_form: Large boss sprite; must be struck in head to defeat
danger_signals.meaning: Ultimate adversary; represents overwhelming physical threat requiring precision and courage

danger_signals.visual_form: Environmental boundary; enemies attempt to throw player character into it
danger_signals.meaning: Death trap and instrument of oppression (references Pharaoh's decree to drown Hebrew infants)

safety_signals.visual_form: Text overlays scattered throughout levels; player collects them
safety_signals.meaning: Health restoration mechanic; spiritual/divine protection; game explicitly ties biblical scripture to character survival

safety_signals.visual_form: Collectible sprites (fruit thrown by monkeys in Noah's Ark)
safety_signals.meaning: Sustenance and resource gathering; represents provision and divine abundance

safety_signals.visual_form: Destination platform/goal area in Noah's Ark segment
safety_signals.meaning: Sanctuary and salvation; physical manifestation of divine protection and covenant

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Biblical narrative identity; player-controlled protagonists from Old Testament
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Temples, religious structures, pastoral settings (sheep herding); collected Bible verses as faction identity

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Enemy soldier sprites; Pharaoh's decree manifested through guard enemies
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Guards with distinct sprite appearance; Goliath as oversized boss entity
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Egyptian settings (Baby Moses); militarized spaces (guards patrolling); oppressive architecture implied

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Real snakes hidden in cave vs. decoy snakes on trees; predatory vs. neutral creatures
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Forests, caves, natural terrain; represents untamed creation and tests of faith

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Animal-filled landscapes; pastoral and natural environments; abundance of creatures to gather
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Peaceful animal collection contrasted with snake decoys; animals must be knocked out or caught non-lethally

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Guard enemy sprites; Nile River as environmental hazard; confined spaces suggesting captivity
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies actively pursue and attempt to throw player into water; protagonist cannot harm enemies—only escape

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Sheep herding environments; natural predators (lions, bears); progression from shepherd to warrior
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Initial avoidance (herding sheep, dodging predators); later progression to combat (sling acquisition, defeating Goliath)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Bible verses scattered as collectible items throughout levels
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Health restoration upon collecting scripture; mechanical linkage between scripture and survival

environmental_storytelling.example: David segment evolves from sheep herding to predator avoidance to guard evasion to final boss battle—mirrors biblical narrative arc from shepherd boy to warrior-king

Baby Moses level: player cannot harm enemies (representing powerlessness under oppression) and must transport the baby character (representing protective responsibility)—mechanic communicates narrative themes of survival under persecution
Noah's Ark: snakes are decoys on trees but real in caves—teaches discernment and references serpent symbolism in biblical narrative
Bible verses function as both mechanical heal items and thematic elements—visually communicates that scripture is protective and restorative in-world
Pharaoh's soldiers and the Nile River create confined, threatening spaces in Baby Moses—visual/spatial design communicates historical captivity narrative

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Carries animals and food to the Ark; active gathering and transportation
Health depletes; requires Bible verses to restore; indicates loss of divine protection
Movement restricted; cannot attack; focus is evasion and safe transport—behavioral limitation mirrors vulnerability under oppression
Actively pursue player; attempt to throw carried character (Moses) into Nile—aggressive, lethal intent
Herds sheep; avoids predators with acorns as non-lethal defense—weak, defensive posture
Dodges guards and environmental hazards; active evasion with ranged capability—gained agency
Engages in direct combat; must strike Goliath's head precisely—peak agency and responsibility
Stationary or limited movement; requires precision strike to head; oversized sprite conveys threat magnitude
Chase or attack player; must be avoided or stunned; represent natural/divine obstacles rather than malicious agency
Throws fruit to player; non-threatening aid—represents provision and assistance

notes: Bible Adventures communicates world-state almost entirely through visual level design, sprite-based threat representation, and mechanical constraint rather than explicit HUD or narrative text (except scattered Bible verses). The three separate biblical narratives create distinct visual/thematic worlds: pastoral (Noah), oppressive (Egypt), and conflict-laden (David). Danger is primarily communicated through enemy sprite design (soldiers, predators) and environmental hazards (water, confined spaces). Safety manifests through collectible scripture and goal structures (the Ark). The game's visual language is deliberately simple (NES limitations) but theologically coherent—mechanics and aesthetics align to communicate themes of divine protection, persecution, and triumphant faith. Political situation (Egyptian oppression) is conveyed through guard enemies and the Nile as execution instrument, not through explicit visual signifiers like banners or architecture.
